blocks.text: Computers store and process everything as **binary** — sequences of 0s and 1s. Text, images, audio, video, code — it all comes down to billions of these two-state signals. Understanding binary is foundational to all of computing.

blocks.title: Why Binary?
blocks.text: Transistors — the fundamental building blocks of CPUs and memory — have two reliable states: **on** (1) and **off** (0). This makes binary natural for electronics. It's also mathematically robust and simple to design circuits around.
Binary is **base-2** — each digit position represents a power of 2, just as each digit in our decimal system represents a power of 10.

blocks.title: Binary conversions in Python
blocks.code:
# Decimal to binary
print(bin(65))     # '0b1000001'
print(bin(255))    # '0b11111111'
print(bin(0))      # '0b0'

# Binary to decimal
print(int('01000001', 2))  # 65
print(int('11111111', 2))  # 255

# Manual conversion
def to_binary(n):
    if n == 0:
        return '0'
    bits = []
    while n > 0:
        bits.append(str(n % 2))
        n //= 2
    return ''.join(reversed(bits))

print(to_binary(65))   # '1000001'
print(to_binary(13))   # '1101'

blocks.text: **Hexadecimal (hex)** is base-16, using digits 0–9 and letters A–F. One hex digit represents exactly 4 binary bits (a nibble), making it a compact way to write binary data.

blocks.title: Hex in Python
blocks.code:
# Decimal to hex
print(hex(255))    # '0xff'
print(hex(16))     # '0x10'
print(hex(65))     # '0x41'

# Hex to decimal
print(int('FF', 16))   # 255
print(int('41', 16))   # 65

# You see hex in: memory addresses, colors, byte dumps
color_red = 0xFF0000  # RGB: Red=255, Green=0, Blue=0
print(color_red)       # 16711680

blocks.title: Where You See Hex
blocks.text: Hex is everywhere in computing: **memory addresses** (`0x7fff5fbff8a0`), **web colors** (`#FF5733`), **cryptographic hashes** (`a3f2...`), **MAC addresses** (`00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E`), file signatures (magic bytes).
How Text Is Stored — ASCII & Unicode
Computers need a standard way to map binary numbers to characters. Two major standards define this:

tabs.label: ASCII
tabs.content: **ASCII** (American Standard Code for Information Interchange, 1963) uses 7 bits to represent 128 characters: uppercase/lowercase letters, digits, punctuation, and control characters. 'A' = 65, 'a' = 97, '0' = 48. ASCII only covers basic English and has no support for accented characters, non-Latin scripts, or emoji.

tabs.label: Unicode / UTF-8
tabs.content: **Unicode** was created to represent every character in every human language — over 140,000 characters. **UTF-8** is the dominant encoding: it uses 1–4 bytes per character. ASCII characters still use 1 byte (backwards compatible). Emoji, Chinese, Arabic, mathematical symbols — all encoded in UTF-8.

blocks.title: Characters and their binary representations
blocks.code:
# ASCII / Unicode values
print(ord('A'))      # 65
print(ord('a'))      # 97
print(ord('0'))      # 48
print(ord('😀'))    # 128512

# Character from number
print(chr(65))       # 'A'
print(chr(128512))   # '😀'

# UTF-8 encoding (bytes)
print('A'.encode('utf-8'))         # b'A'  (1 byte)
print('é'.encode('utf-8'))         # b'\xc3\xa9' (2 bytes)
print('中'.encode('utf-8'))        # b'\xe4\xb8\xad' (3 bytes)
print('😀'.encode('utf-8'))       # b'\xf0\x9f\x98\x80' (4 bytes)

items.title: Integers — Two's Complement
items.content: Signed integers use **two's complement** to represent negative numbers. In an 8-bit integer: 0–127 are positive (0000 0000 to 0111 1111), and 128–255 represent -128 to -1 (1000 0000 to 1111 1111). To negate a number: flip all bits, then add 1. This makes arithmetic circuits simple — the same ADD instruction works for positive and negative numbers.

items.title: Floating-Point — IEEE 754
items.content: Real numbers (like 3.14) are stored using the **IEEE 754** standard. A 32-bit float has: 1 sign bit + 8 exponent bits + 23 mantissa bits. This allows a huge range of values but with limited precision. This is why `0.1 + 0.2 !== 0.3` in most languages — it's a base-2 representation issue, not a bug.

items.title: Boolean — 1 or 0
items.content: A boolean (true/false) needs only 1 bit logically, but is typically stored as 1 byte for memory alignment reasons. In C: `true` = 1, `false` = 0. Logical operators AND, OR, NOT, XOR operate on individual bits and entire integers (bitwise operations).

points: All data in a computer is stored as binary (0s and 1s)
1 byte = 8 bits. One hex digit represents 4 bits
ASCII maps 128 characters to 7-bit codes; Unicode/UTF-8 supports all of humanity's scripts
Integers use two's complement for signed representation; floats use IEEE 754
Hex is used as a human-readable shorthand for binary in addresses, colors, and byte dumps

questions.question: What is the decimal value of the binary number 1101?
Why does 0.1 + 0.2 not equal exactly 0.3 in most programming languages?
options: It's a bug in all programming languages
Floating-point numbers use base-2, which cannot represent 0.1 exactly
The CPU rounds to the nearest integer
questions.explanation: 0.1 and 0.2 cannot be represented exactly in binary (base-2) floating-point, similar to how 1/3 cannot be expressed exactly in decimal. The IEEE 754 standard stores an approximation, leading to small rounding errors.
